E. He decides to confront the town engineer about the broken pipes.

evidence_dialogues.speaker: Aslaksen

session_2.speaker: NARRATION
session_2.text: (SCENE.—The same. The door into the dining room is shut. It is morning. MRS. STOCKMANN, with a sealed letter in her hand, comes in from the dining room, goes to the door of the DOCTOR'S study, and peeps in.)

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Are you in, Thomas?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, I have just come in. What is it?

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: A letter from your brother.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Aha, let us see! I return herewith the manuscript you sent me H'm!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: What does he say?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh, he only writes that he will come up here himself about midday.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Well, try and remember to be at home this time.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: That will be all right; I have got through all my morning visits.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: I am extremely curious to know how he takes it.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: You will see he won't like it's having been I, and not he, that made the discovery.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Aren't you a little nervous about that?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh, he really will be pleased enough, you know. But, at the same time, Peter is so confoundedly afraid of anyone's doing any service to the town except himself.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: I will tell you what, Thomas—you should be good natured, and share the credit of this with him. Couldn't you make out that it was he who set you on the scent of this discovery?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I am quite willing. If only I can get the thing set right. I—

session_2.speaker: STAGE
session_2.text: (MORTEN KIIL puts his head in through the door leading from the hall, looks around in an enquiring manner, and chuckles.)

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Is it—is it true?

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Father!—is it you?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Ah, Mr. Kiil—good morning, good morning!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: But come along in.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: If it is true, I will; if not, I am off.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: If what is true?

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: This tale about the water supply, is it true?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Certainly it is true, but how did you come to hear it?

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Petra ran in on her way to the school—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Did she?

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Yes; and she declares that—I thought she was only making a fool of me—but it isn't like Petra to do that.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Of course not. How could you imagine such a thing!

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Oh well, it is better never to trust anybody; you may find you have been made a fool of before you know where you are. But it is really true, all the same?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: You can depend upon it that it is true. Won't you sit down? Isn't it a real bit of luck for the town—

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: A bit of luck for the town?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, that I made the discovery in good time.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Yes, yes. Yes!—But I should never have thought you the sort of man to pull your own brother's leg like this!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Pull his leg!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Really, father dear—

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Let me see, what was the story? Some kind of beast that had got into the water-pipes, wasn't it?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Infusoria—yes.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: And a lot of these beasts had got in, according to Petra—a tremendous lot.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Certainly; hundreds of thousands of them, probably.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: But no one can see them—isn't that so?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes; you can't see them,

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Damn—it's the finest story I have ever heard!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: What do you mean?

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: But you will never get the Mayor to believe a thing like that.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: We shall see.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Do you think he will be fool enough to—?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I hope the whole town will be fools enough.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: The whole town! Well, it wouldn't be a bad thing. It would just serve them right, and teach them a lesson. They think themselves so much cleverer than we old fellows. They hounded me out of the council; they did, I tell you—they hounded me out. Now they shall pay for it. You pull their legs too, Thomas!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Really, I—

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: You pull their legs! If you can work it so that the Mayor and his friends all swallow the same bait, I will give ten pounds to a charity—like a shot!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: That is very kind of you.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Yes, I haven't got much money to throw away, I can tell you; but, if you can work this, I will give five pounds to a charity at Christmas.

session_2.speaker: STAGE
session_2.text: (HOVSTAD comes in by the hall door.)

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Good morning! Oh, I beg your pardon

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Not at all; come in.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: Oho!—is he in this too?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: What do you mean?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Certainly he is.

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: I might have known it! It must get into the papers. You know how to do it, Thomas! Set your wits to work. Now I must go.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Won't you stay a little while?

session_2.speaker: Morten Kiil
session_2.text: No, I must be off now. You keep up this game for all it is worth; you won't repent it, I'm damned if you will!

session_2.speaker: STAGE
session_2.text: (He goes out; MRS. STOCKMANN follows him into the hall.)

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Just imagine—the old chap doesn't believe a word of all this about the water supply.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Oh that was it, then?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, that was what we were talking about. Perhaps it is the same thing that brings you here?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Yes, it is, Can you spare me a few minutes, Doctor?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: As long as you like, my dear fellow.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Have you heard from the Mayor yet?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Not yet. He is coming here later.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: I have given the matter a great deal of thought since last night.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Well?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: From your point of view, as a doctor and a man of science, this affair of the water supply is an isolated matter. I mean, you do not realise that it involves a great many other things.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: How do you mean?—Let us sit down, my dear fellow. No, sit here on the couch. Now then. You mean that?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: You said yesterday that the pollution of the water was due to impurities in the soil.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, unquestionably it is due to that poisonous morass up at Molledal.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Begging your pardon, Doctor, I fancy it is due to quite another morass altogether.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: What morass?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: The morass that the whole life of our town is built on and is rotting in.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: What the deuce are you driving at, Hovstad?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: The whole of the town's interests have, little by little, got into the hands of a pack of officials.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh, come!—they are not all officials.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: No, but those that are not officials are at any rate the officials' friends and adherents; it is the wealthy folk, the old families in the town, that have got us entirely in their hands.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, but after all they are men of ability and knowledge.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Did they show any ability or knowledge when they laid the conduit pipes where they are now?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: No, of course that was a great piece of stupidity on their part. But that is going to be set right now.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Do you think that will be all such plain sailing?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Plain sailing or no, it has got to be done, anyway.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Yes, provided the press takes up the question.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I don't think that will be necessary, my dear fellow, I am certain my brother—

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Excuse me, doctor; I feel bound to tell you I am inclined to take the matter up.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: In the paper?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Yes. When I took over the "People's Messenger" my idea was to break up this ring of self-opinionated old fossils who had got hold of all the influence.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: But you know you told me yourself what the result had been; you nearly ruined your paper.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Yes, at the time we were obliged to climb down a peg or two, it is quite true—because there was a danger of the whole project of the Baths coming to nothing if they failed us. But now the scheme has been carried through, and we can dispense with these grand gentlemen.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Dispense with them, yes; but, we owe them a great debt of gratitude.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: That shall be recognised ungrudgingly, But a journalist of my democratic tendencies cannot let such an opportunity as this slip. The bubble of official infallibility must be pricked. This superstition must be destroyed, like any other.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I am whole-heartedly with you in that, Mr. Hovstad; if it is a superstition, away with it!

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: I should be very reluctant to bring the Mayor into it, because he is your brother. But I am sure you will agree with me that truth should be the first consideration.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: That goes without saying. Yes, but—but—

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: You must not misjudge me. I am neither more self-interested nor more ambitious than most men.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: My dear fellow—who suggests anything of the kind?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: I am of humble origin, as you know; and that has given me opportunities of knowing what is the most crying need in the humbler ranks of life. It is that they should be allowed some part in the direction of public affairs, Doctor. That is what will develop their faculties and intelligence and self respect—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I quite appreciate that.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Yes—and in my opinion a journalist incurs a heavy responsibility if he neglects a favourable opportunity of emancipating the masses—the humble and oppressed. I know well enough that in exalted circles I shall be called an agitator, and all that sort of thing; but they may call what they like. If only my conscience doesn't reproach me, then—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Quite right! Quite right, Mr. Hovstad. But all the same—devil take it! Come in!

session_2.speaker: STAGE
session_2.text: (ASLAKSEN appears at the door. He is poorly but decently dressed, in black, with a slightly crumpled white neckcloth; he wears gloves and has a felt hat in his hand.)

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Excuse my taking the liberty, Doctor—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Ah, it is you, Aslaksen!

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Yes, Doctor.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Is it me you want, Aslaksen?

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: No; I didn't know I should find you here. No, it was the Doctor I—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I am quite at your service. What is it?

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Is what I heard from Mr. Billing true, sir—that you mean to improve our water supply?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, for the Baths.

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Quite so, I understand. Well, I have come to say that I will back that up by every means in my power.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: You see!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I shall be very grateful to you, but—

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Because it may be no bad thing to have us small tradesmen at your back. We form, as it were, a compact majority in the town—if we choose. And it is always a good thing to have the majority with you, Doctor.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: That is undeniably true; but I confess I don't see why such unusual precautions should be necessary in this case. It seems to me that such a plain, straightforward thing—

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Oh, it may be very desirable, all the same. I know our local authorities so well; officials are not generally very ready to act on proposals that come from other people. That is why I think it would not be at all amiss if we made a little demonstration.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: That's right.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Demonstration, did you say? What on earth are you going to make a demonstration about?

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: We shall proceed with the greatest moderation, Doctor. Moderation is always my aim; it is the greatest virtue in a citizen—at least, I think so.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: It is well known to be a characteristic of yours, Mr. Aslaksen.

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Yes, I think I may pride myself on that. And this matter of the water supply is of the greatest importance to us small tradesmen. The Baths promise to be a regular gold-mine for the town. We shall all make our living out of them, especially those of us who are householder. That is why we will back up the project as strongly as possible. And as I am at present Chairman of the Householders' Association.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes—?

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: And, what is more, local secretary of the Temperance Society—you know, sir, I suppose, that I am a worker in the temperance cause?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Of course, of course.

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Well, you can understand that I come into contact with a great many people. And as I have the reputation of a temperate and law-abiding citizen—like yourself, Doctor—I have a certain influence in the town, a little bit of power, if I may be allowed to say so.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I know that quite well, Mr. Aslaksen.

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: So you see it would be an easy matter for me to set on foot some testimonial, if necessary.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: A testimonial?

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Yes, some kind of an address of thanks from the townsmen for your share in a matter of such importance to the community. I need scarcely say that it would have to be drawn up with the greatest regard to moderation, so as not to offend the authorities—who, after all, have the reins in their hands. If we pay strict attention to that, no one can take it amiss, I should think!

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Well, and even supposing they didn't like it—

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: No, no, no; there must be no discourtesy to the authorities, Mr. Hovstad. It is no use falling foul of those upon whom our welfare so closely depends. I have done that in my time, and no good ever comes of it. But no one can take exception to a reasonable and frank expression of a citizen's views.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I can't tell you, dear Mr. Aslaksen, how extremely pleased I am to find such hearty support among my fellow-citizens. I am delighted—delighted! Now, you will take a small glass of sherry, eh?

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: No, thank you; I never drink alcohol of that kind.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Well, what do you say to a glass of beer, then?

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Nor that either, thank you, Doctor. I never drink anything as early as this. I am going into town now to talk this over with one or two householders, and prepare the ground.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: It is tremendously kind of you, Mr. Aslaksen; but I really cannot understand the necessity for all these precautions. It seems to me that the thing should go of itself.

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: The authorities are somewhat slow to move, Doctor. Far be it from me to seem to blame them—

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: We are going to stir them up in the paper tomorrow, Aslaksen.

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: But not violently, I trust, Mr. Hovstad. Proceed with moderation, or you will do nothing with them. You may take my advice; I have gathered my experience in the school of life. Well, I must say goodbye, Doctor. You know now that we small tradesmen are at your back at all events, like a solid wall. You have the compact majority on your side Doctor.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I am very much obliged, dear Mr. Aslaksen, Goodbye, goodbye.

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Are you going my way, towards the printing-office. Mr. Hovstad?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: I will come later; I have something to settle up first.

session_2.speaker: Aslaksen
session_2.text: Very well.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Well, what do you think of that, Doctor? Don't you think it is high time we stirred a little life into all this slackness and vacillation and cowardice?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Are you referring to Aslaksen?

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Yes, I am. He is one of those who are floundering in a bog—decent enough fellow though he may be, otherwise. And most of the people here are in just the same case—see-sawing and edging first to one side and then to the other, so overcome with caution and scruple that they never dare to take any decided step.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, but Aslaksen seemed to me so thoroughly well-intentioned.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: There is one thing I esteem higher than that; and that is for a man to be self-reliant and sure of himself.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I think you are perfectly right there.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: That is why I want to seize this opportunity, and try if I cannot manage to put a little virility into these well-intentioned people for once. The idol of Authority must be shattered in this town. This gross and inexcusable blunder about the water supply must be brought home to the mind of every municipal voter.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Very well; if you are of opinion that it is for the good of the community, so be it. But not until I have had a talk with my brother.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Anyway, I will get a leading article ready; and if the Mayor refuses to take the matter up—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: How can you suppose such a thing possible!

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: It is conceivable. And in that case—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: In that case I promise you—. Look here, in that case you may print my report—every word of it.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: May I? Have I your word for it?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Here it is; take it with you. It can do no harm for you to read it through, and you can give it me back later on.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Good, good! That is what I will do. And now goodbye, Doctor.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Goodbye, goodbye. You will see everything will run quite smoothly, Mr. Hovstad—quite smoothly.

session_2.speaker: Hovstad
session_2.text: Hm!—we shall see.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Katherine! Oh, you are back, Petra?

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Yes, I have just come from the school.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Has he not been here yet?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Peter? No, but I have had a long talk with Hovstad. He is quite excited about my discovery, I find it has a much wider bearing than I at first imagined. And he has put his paper at my disposal if necessity should arise.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Do you think it will?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Not for a moment. But at all events it makes me feel proud to know that I have the liberal-minded independent press on my side. Yes, and just imagine—I have had a visit from the Chairman of the Householders' Association!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh! What did he want?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: To offer me his support too. They will support me in a body if it should be necessary. Katherine—do you know what I have got behind me?

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Behind you? No, what have you got behind you?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: The compact majority.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Really? Is that a good thing for you Thomas?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I should think it was a good thing. By Jove, it's a fine thing to feel this bond of brotherhood between oneself and one's fellow citizens!

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: And to be able to do so much that is good and useful, father!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And for one's own native town into the bargain, my child!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: That was a ring at the bell.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: It must be he, then. Come in!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Good morning.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Glad to see you, Peter!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Good morning, Peter, How are you?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: So so, thank you. I received from you yesterday, after office hours, a report dealing with the condition of the water at the Baths.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes. Have you read it?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, I have,

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And what have you to say to it?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Hm!—

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Come along, Petra.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Was it necessary to make all these investigations behind my back?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, because until I was absolutely certain about it—

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Then you mean that you are absolutely certain now?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Surely you are convinced of that.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Is it your intention to bring this document before the Baths Committee as a sort of official communication?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Certainly. Something must be done in the matter—and that quickly.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: As usual, you employ violent expressions in your report. You say, amongst other things, that what we offer visitors in our Baths is a permanent supply of poison.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Well, can you describe it any other way, Peter? Just think—water that is poisonous, whether you drink it or bathe in it! And this we offer to the poor sick folk who come to us trustfully and pay us at an exorbitant rate to be made well again!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: And your reasoning leads you to this conclusion, that we must build a sewer to draw off the alleged impurities from Molledal and must relay the water conduits.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes. Do you see any other way out of it? I don't.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: I made a pretext this morning to go and see the town engineer, and, as if only half seriously, broached the subject of these proposals as a thing we might perhaps have to take under consideration some time later on.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Some time later on!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: He smiled at what he considered to be my extravagance, naturally. Have you taken the trouble to consider what your proposed alterations would cost? According to the information I obtained, the expenses would probably mount up to fifteen or twenty thousand pounds.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Would it cost so much?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes; and the worst part of it would be that the work would take at least two years.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Two years? Two whole years?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: At least. And what are we to do with the Baths in the meantime? Close them? Indeed we should be obliged to. And do you suppose anyone would come near the place after it had got out that the water was dangerous?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes but, Peter, that is what it is.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: And all this at this juncture—just as the Baths are beginning to be known. There are other towns in the neighbourhood with qualifications to attract visitors for bathing purposes. Don't you suppose they would immediately strain every nerve to divert the entire stream of strangers to themselves? Unquestionably they would; and then where should we be? We should probably have to abandon the whole thing, which has cost us so much money-and then you would have ruined your native town.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I—should have ruined—!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: It is simply and solely through the Baths that the town has before it any future worth mentioning. You know that just as well as I.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: But what do you think ought to be done, then?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Your report has not convinced me that the condition of the water at the Baths is as bad as you represent it to be.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I tell you it is even worse!—or at all events it will be in summer, when the warm weather comes.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: As I said, I believe you exaggerate the matter considerably. A capable physician ought to know what measures to take—he ought to be capable of preventing injurious influences or of remedying them if they become obviously persistent.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Well? What more?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: The water supply for the Baths is now an established fact, and in consequence must be treated as such. But probably the Committee, at its discretion, will not be disinclined to consider the question of how far it might be possible to introduce certain improvements consistently with a reasonable expenditure.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And do you suppose that I will have anything to do with such a piece of trickery as that?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Trickery!!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, it would be a trick—a fraud, a lie, a downright crime towards the public, towards the whole community!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: I have not, as I remarked before, been able to convince myself that there is actually any imminent danger.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: You have! It is impossible that you should not be convinced. I know I have represented the facts absolutely truthfully and fairly. And you know it very well, Peter, only you won't acknowledge it. It was owing to your action that both the Baths and the water conduits were built where they are; and that is what you won't acknowledge—that damnable blunder of yours. Pooh!—do you suppose I don't see through you?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: And even if that were true? If I perhaps guard my reputation somewhat anxiously, it is in the interests of the town. Without moral authority I am powerless to direct public affairs as seems, to my judgment, to be best for the common good. And on that account—and for various other reasons too—it appears to me to be a matter of importance that your report should not be delivered to the Committee. In the interests of the public, you must withhold it. Then, later on, I will raise the question and we will do our best, privately; but nothing of this unfortunate affair not a single word of it—must come to the ears of the public.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I am afraid you will not be able to prevent that now, my dear Peter.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: It must and shall be prevented.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: It is no use, I tell you. There are too many people that know about it.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: That know about it? Who? Surely you don't mean those fellows on the "People's Messenger"?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, they know. The liberal-minded independent press is going to see that you do your duty.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: You are an extraordinarily independent man, Thomas. Have you given no thought to the consequences this may have for yourself?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Consequences?—for me?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: For you and yours, yes.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: What the deuce do you mean?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: I believe I have always behaved in a brotherly way to you—haven't I always been ready to oblige or to help you?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, you have, and I am grateful to you for it.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: There is no need. Indeed, to some extent I was forced to do so—for my own sake. I always hoped that, if I helped to improve your financial position, I should be able to keep some check on you.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: What! Then it was only for your own sake—!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Up to a certain point, yes. It is painful for a man in an official position to have his nearest relative compromising himself time after time.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And do you consider that I do that?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, unfortunately, you do, without even being aware of it. You have a restless, pugnacious, rebellious disposition. And then there is that disastrous propensity of yours to want to write about every sort of possible and impossible thing. The moment an idea comes into your head, you must needs go and write a newspaper article or a whole pamphlet about it.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Well, but is it not the duty of a citizen to let the public share in any new ideas he may have?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh, the public doesn't require any new ideas. The public is best served by the good, old established ideas it already has.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And that is your honest opinion?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, and for once I must talk frankly to you. Hitherto I have tried to avoid doing so, because I know how irritable you are; but now I must tell you the truth, Thomas. You have no conception what an amount of harm you do yourself by your impetuosity. You complain of the authorities, you even complain of the government—you are always pulling them to pieces; you insist that you have been neglected and persecuted. But what else can such a cantankerous man as you expect?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: What next! Cantankerous, am I?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, Thomas, you are an extremely cantankerous man to work with—I know that to my cost. You disregard everything that you ought to have consideration for. You seem completely to forget that it is me you have to thank for your appointment here as medical officer to the Baths.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I was entitled to it as a matter of course!—I and nobody else! I was the first person to see that the town could be made into a flourishing watering-place, and I was the only one who saw it at that time. I had to fight single-handed in support of the idea for many years; and I wrote and wrote—

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Undoubtedly. But things were not ripe for the scheme then—though, of course, you could not judge of that in your out-of-the-way corner up north. But as soon as the opportune moment came I—and the others—took the matter into our hands.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, and made this mess of all my beautiful plan. It is pretty obvious now what clever fellows you were!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: To my mind the whole thing only seems to mean that you are seeking another outlet for your combativeness. You want to pick a quarrel with your superiors—an old habit of yours. You cannot put up with any authority over you. You look askance at anyone who occupies a superior official position; you regard him as a personal enemy, and then any stick is good enough to beat him with. But now I have called your attention to the fact that the town's interests are at stake—and, incidentally, my own too. And therefore, I must tell you, Thomas, that you will find me inexorable with regard to what I am about to require you to do.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And what is that?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: As you have been so indiscreet as to speak of this delicate matter to outsiders, despite the fact that you ought to have treated it as entirely official and confidential, it is obviously impossible to hush it up now. All sorts of rumours will get about directly, and everybody who has a grudge against us will take care to embellish these rumours. So it will be necessary for you to refute them publicly.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I! How? I don't understand.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: What we shall expect is that, after making further investigations, you will come to the conclusion that the matter is not by any means as dangerous or as critical as you imagined in the first instance.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Oho!—so that is what you expect!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: And, what is more, we shall expect you to make public profession of your confidence in the Committee and in their readiness to consider fully and conscientiously what steps may be necessary to remedy any possible defects.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: But you will never be able to do that by patching and tinkering at it—never! Take my word for it, Peter; I mean what I say, as deliberately and emphatically as possible.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: As an officer under the Committee, you have no right to any individual opinion.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: No right?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: In your official capacity, no. As a private person, it is quite another matter. But as a subordinate member of the staff of the Baths, you have no right to express any opinion which runs contrary to that of your superiors.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: This is too much! I, a doctor, a man of science, have no right to—!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: The matter in hand is not simply a scientific one. It is a complicated matter, and has its economic as well as its technical side.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I don't care what it is! I intend to be free to express my opinion on any subject under the sun.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: As you please—but not on any subject concerning the Baths. That we forbid.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: You forbid—! You! A pack of—

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: I forbid it—I, your chief; and if I forbid it, you have to obey.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Peter—if you were not my brother—

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Father, you shan't stand this!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Petra, Petra!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh, so you have been eavesdropping.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: You were talking so loud, we couldn't help it!

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Yes, I was listening.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Well, after all, I am very glad—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: You were saying something about forbidding and obeying?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: You obliged me to take that tone with you.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And so I am to give myself the lie, publicly?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: We consider it absolutely necessary that you should make some such public statement as I have asked for.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And if I do not—obey?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Then we shall publish a statement ourselves to reassure the public.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Very well; but in that case I shall use my pen against you. I stick to what I have said; I will show that I am right and that you are wrong. And what will you do then?

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Then I shall not be able to prevent your being dismissed.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: What—?

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Father—dismissed!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Dismissed!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Dismissed from the staff of the Baths. I shall be obliged to propose that you shall immediately be given notice, and shall not be allowed any further participation in the Baths' affairs.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: You would dare to do that!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: It is you that are playing the daring game.

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Uncle, that is a shameful way to treat a man like father!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Do hold your tongue, Petra!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh, so we volunteer our opinions already, do we? Of course. Katherine, I imagine you are the most sensible person in this house. Use any influence you may have over your husband, and make him see what this will entail for his family as well as—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: My family is my own concern and nobody else's!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: —for his own family, as I was saying, as well as for the town he lives in.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: It is I who have the real good of the town at heart! I want to lay bare the defects that sooner or later must come to the light of day. I will show whether I love my native town.

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: You, who in your blind obstinacy want to cut off the most important source of the town's welfare?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: The source is poisoned, man! Are you mad? We are making our living by retailing filth and corruption! The whole of our flourishing municipal life derives its sustenance from a lie!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: All imagination—or something even worse. The man who can throw out such offensive insinuations about his native town must be an enemy to our community.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Do you dare to—!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Thomas!

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Don't lose your temper, father!

session_2.speaker: Peter Stockmann
session_2.text: I will not expose myself to violence. Now you have had a warning; so reflect on what you owe to yourself and your family. Goodbye.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Am I to put up with such treatment as this? In my own house, Katherine! What do you think of that!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Indeed it is both shameful and absurd, Thomas—

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: If only I could give uncle a piece of my mind—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: It is my own fault. I ought to have flown out at him long ago!—shown my teeth!—bitten! To hear him call me an enemy to our community! Me! I shall not take that lying down, upon my soul!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: But, dear Thomas, your brother has power on his side.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, but I have right on mine, I tell you.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh yes, right—right. What is the use of having right on your side if you have not got might?

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Oh, mother!—how can you say such a thing!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Do you imagine that in a free country it is no use having right on your side? You are absurd, Katherine. Besides, haven't I got the liberal-minded, independent press to lead the way, and the compact majority behind me? That is might enough, I should think!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: But, good heavens, Thomas, you don't mean to?

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Don't mean to what?

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: To set yourself up in opposition to your brother.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: In God's name, what else do you suppose I should do but take my stand on right and truth?

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Yes, I was just going to say that.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: But it won't do you any earthly good. If they won't do it, they won't.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: Oho, Katherine! Just give me time, and you will see how I will carry the war into their camp.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, you carry the war into their camp, and you get your dismissal—that is what you will do.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: In any case I shall have done my duty towards the public—towards the community, I, who am called its enemy!

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: But towards your family, Thomas? Towards your own home! Do you think that is doing your duty towards those you have to provide for?

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Ah, don't think always first of us, mother.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Oh, it is easy for you to talk; you are able to shift for yourself, if need be. But remember the boys, Thomas; and think a little of yourself too, and of me—

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I think you are out of your senses, Katherine! If I were to be such a miserable coward as to go on my knees to Peter and his damned crew, do you suppose I should ever know an hour's peace of mind all my life afterwards?

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: I don't know anything about that; but God preserve us from the peace of mind we shall have, all the same, if you go on defying him! You will find yourself again without the means of subsistence, with no income to count upon. I should think we had had enough of that in the old days. Remember that, Thomas; think what that means.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: And this is what this slavery can bring upon a free, honourable man! Isn't it horrible, Katherine?

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Yes, it is sinful to treat you so, it is perfectly true. But, good heavens, one has to put up with so much injustice in this world. There are the boys, Thomas! Look at them! What is to become of them? Oh, no, no, you can never have the heart—.

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: The boys— I No, even if the whole world goes to pieces, I will never bow my neck to this yokel

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: Thomas—what are you going to do!

session_2.speaker: Dr. Stockmann
session_2.text: I mean to have the right to look my sons in the face when they are grown men.

session_2.speaker: Mrs. Stockmann
session_2.text: God help us all!

session_2.speaker: Petra
session_2.text: Father is splendid! He will not give in.

session_2.speaker: STAGE
session_2.text: (The boys look on in amazement; PETRA signs to them not to speak.)
